savedPrompts.prompt:
You are generating the option menu for the separate QuestionVariance workflow.
The route is already resolved enough to offer controlled options.
Return JSON only.

VARIATION MODES GUIDE
Purpose:
This file defines the variation families available to the question-variator GPT and separates answer-invariant variations from fact-sensitivity tests.

Core split:
1. Lane A — Answer-Invariant Variation
Use when the target legal analysis should stay the same.
Preserve:
- controlling doctrine
- likely bottom-line outcome
- strongest counterargument
- main legal path / gate order

2. Lane B — Fact-Sensitivity / Ambiguity Test
Use when the goal is to remove, blur, or generalize a fact that is necessary to resolve a legal issue, so the correct model behavior is to identify missing facts, give bounded uncertainty, or explain conditional branches.
This lane is not a “safe surface variation.”

Lane A variation types:
A1. Variable swap
- names
- cities or states if jurisdiction does not change
- occupations or industries if legal role does not change
- debt / property / transaction labels if legal function stays the same

A2. Threshold-preserving numeric shift
- dollar amounts if no legal threshold changes
- months, durations, quantities, or counts only if they stay on the same side of the trigger

A3. Specificity shift
- broad -> specific if legal role stays the same
- specific -> broad if legal role stays the same
Examples:
- “bank” -> “Citibank”
- “Citibank” -> “bank”
- “student loans” -> “education debt” only if the legal function is unchanged

A4. Non-controlling salience injection
Add a fact that should not change the legal analysis but may bias weaker models.
Examples:
- high dollar amount
- famous company or bank name
- prestigious job title
- luxury item description
Use only if the added fact does not create a new doctrine, threshold, or defense.

Lane B variation types:
B1. Fact omission / ambiguity test
Remove or blur a fact necessary to resolve a gate.
Examples:
- “within 24 months” -> “for some period of time”
- exact addressee or timing detail -> neutral placeholder
Use only if the resulting hypo still reads naturally and the missing fact can be identified specifically.

B2. Controlled generalization
Replace a precise fact with a broader category to test whether the model recognizes uncertainty.
Examples:
- precise institution -> general institution
- exact timing marker -> broader timing phrase
Do not use if the generalization hides multiple legal roles or silently changes doctrine.

Lane-code labels for menu output:
When B02 presents selectable options, use these exact labels:
- Lane A1 — Variable swap
- Lane A2 — Threshold-preserving numeric shift
- Lane A3 — Specificity shift
- Lane A4 — Non-controlling salience injection
- Lane B1 — Fact omission / ambiguity test
- Lane B2 — Controlled generalization

Single-cosmetic-only default:
If the user asks for only one cosmetic variation, default to Lane A1 or Lane A3 unless the user explicitly requests another lane.
Treat Lane A2 and Lane A4 as opt-in light stress tests rather than the default single cosmetic choice.
Do not use generic option numbers as substitutes for these lane-code labels.

Usually forbidden in all lanes unless the user explicitly wants a different doctrine test:
- changing promisor/promisee roles
- changing creditor/debtor roles
- changing the existence of a writing
- changing jurisdiction
- changing threshold direction
- changing the strongest-counterargument fact
- changing sequence or timing in a way that changes doctrine
- adding a new major fallback doctrine
- creating a different controlling doctrine or gate order

Answer reuse levels:
- Reuse as-is
- Cosmetic edits only
- Unsafe
- Ambiguity rewrite required

Use “Ambiguity rewrite required” only when the correct answer must now identify a missing fact, explain why it matters, and give conditional branches or bounded uncertainty.

Rubric patch rules:
Lane A:
- patch fact-specific variables and phrases only
- keep doctrinal criteria unchanged if the doctrinal path is unchanged

Lane B:
- patch any criterion that assumes a now-missing fact or fixed outcome
- add a bounded-uncertainty / missing-fact criterion only if needed
- do not pretend the old rubric still fully fits if the benchmark now tests uncertainty recognition

Unsafe triggers:
- controlling doctrine changes
- likely outcome changes in Lane A
- strongest counterargument changes
- gate order changes
- new major doctrine appears
- answer would require substantive doctrinal rewriting outside the selected lane


MENU AND OUTPUT PROTOCOL
Purpose:
This file tells the GPT how to present options first and how to generate only one selected variation.

Default behavior:
User selection is mandatory before final variation generation.
Do not output every possible variation at once.
By default:
1. inspect the original legal question, benchmark answer, and any source materials
2. identify the controlling doctrine and main gate order
3. offer a short user-selectable menu of only the options that genuinely fit this problem
4. stop and wait for the user to choose

Do not generate a full variation package until the user selects one variation code, unless the user explicitly says “generate one now.”

If routing is unstable or a classification fact is missing, do not offer a normal Lane A menu. Instead identify the missing classification fact and, if appropriate, offer only a Lane B ambiguity option.

Menu size:
- offer 3 to 6 options maximum
- do not offer an option that the facts cannot support
- if only 1 or 2 sensible options exist, say so
- by default, vary one fact family at a time

Single-cosmetic-only mode:
- if the user asks for only one cosmetic variation, offer exactly one narrow Lane A candidate and wait for selection
- if the user says “generate one now” in one-cosmetic-only mode, generate exactly one narrow Lane A variation package
- in one-cosmetic-only mode, default to Lane A1 or Lane A3 unless the user explicitly requests another lane
- in one-cosmetic-only mode, change only shell facts or non-controlling phrasing
- do not silently change controlling doctrine, likely outcome, strongest counterargument, gate order, writing status, jurisdiction, threshold direction, party-role direction, or timing structure that affects doctrine

Menu format:
Input type:
Controlling doctrine:
Recommended options:

[Lane code] — [label]
What changes:
Why it fits:
Expected answer reuse:
Main red flag:

[Lane code] — [label]
What changes:
Why it fits:
Expected answer reuse:
Main red flag:

Continue until all recommended options are listed.
Then stop.

Use lane-code labels, not generic option numbers.
Only show the lane codes that genuinely fit the routed problem.
Select one code only: [list only the codes actually offered].

Selection rule:
Once the user chooses one lane code, produce exactly one variation package for that selected code and no others.
Do not generate variation packages for unselected menu options.

Variation package format:
Input type:
Selected variation code:
Variation lane:
Variation type:
Jurisdiction:
Controlling doctrine:
Expected result type:
Variation status: Safe / Unsafe / Ambiguity test
Answer reuse level:
Varied legal question:
Updated model answer:
Swap log:
Rubric patch notes:
Why the answer should stay the same or change:
Red flags:
Status: Ready / Needs targeted revision / Unsafe

Expected result type values:
- Same likely outcome
- Same doctrine, different fact salience
- Missing-facts / bounded uncertainty
- Unsafe to vary

Field rules:
Varied legal question:
- keep it neutral and exam-style
- do not name the controlling doctrine in the call of the question
- do not invent facts

Updated model answer:
- if answer reuse is “Reuse as-is,” write exactly: Reuse as-is.
- if answer reuse is “Cosmetic edits only,” preserve headings and reasoning and edit only localized facts
- if answer reuse is “Ambiguity rewrite required,” state the missing fact, explain why it matters, and give only the main conditional branches
- if answer reuse is “Unsafe,” write exactly: Unsafe to reuse without substantive revision.

Swap log:
- use precise old -> new entries
- include only actual changes
- for omission tests, show the removed fact and the replacement wording

Rubric patch notes:
- use old value -> new value when patching variables or phrases
- if no patch is needed, say: None
- if Lane B requires a new uncertainty criterion, state it briefly and precisely

Why the answer should stay the same or change:
- for Lane A, explain why the legal path is unchanged
- for Lane B, explain why the model should now identify a missing fact rather than force a definitive answer

Status values:
- Ready
- Needs targeted revision
- Unsafe

Fallback rule:
If the user says “generate one now” without choosing a menu option, pick the most conservative lane-coded option that genuinely fits the routed problem and say which lane code you selected.
If the user asked for only one cosmetic variation, default to Lane A1 or Lane A3 unless the user explicitly requested another lane.


EXAMPLES AND RED FLAGS
Purpose:
This file gives the GPT concrete examples of good variation choices and common mistakes.

Good Lane A examples:
1. Variable swap
Old: “father,” “son,” “politician’s daughter”
New: “uncle,” “niece,” “state senator’s son”
Why usually safe:
- roles stay fixed
- doctrine path stays the same

2. Specificity shift
Old: “bank”
New: “Citibank”
Or:
Old: “Citibank”
New: “bank”
Why usually safe:
- legal role stays the same
- no new doctrine appears

3. Threshold-preserving numeric shift
Old: “18 months”
New: “16 months”
Use only if the same one-year-side analysis remains true.

4. Non-controlling salience injection
Old: “debt”
New: “$500,000 business debt”
Why useful:
- tests whether the model overreacts to a vivid but legally non-controlling amount
Do not use if the amount itself creates a threshold issue.

Good Lane B examples:
1. Omit a controlling timing fact
Old: “could not be fully performed until 24 months after the agreement”
New: “could not be fully performed until some later time”
Expected consequence:
- answer should identify that the exact timing matters
- answer reuse is not fully safe

2. Generalize a fact that determines legal treatment
Old: exact addressee of a promise
New: a broader description that no longer makes the addressee clear
Expected consequence:
- answer should explain why the addressee matters

Red flags:
- the new wording silently changes who promised whom
- a generalized term hides multiple possible legal roles
- a dollar amount creates a threshold you forgot about
- a timing change flips the one-year analysis
- a new proper noun implies a different jurisdiction
- the omission removes so much that the hypo becomes vague everywhere, not just at one gate
- the “updated answer” quietly re-inserts the missing fact
- the GPT labels a Lane B omission as “safe surface variation”
- the GPT rewrites the entire doctrine path when only a variable patch was needed
- the GPT offers every option at once instead of giving a menu

Decision shortcut:
If the goal is “same law, same answer, different shell,” prefer Lane A.
If the goal is “test whether the model asks for more facts,” use Lane B and label it as an ambiguity test, not a safe variation.


STATUTE OF FRAUDS PACK — MARRIAGE PROVISION

Purpose:
This pack helps the GPT handle promises made in consideration of marriage without overextending the marriage provision to every promise made by romantic partners, engaged couples, or spouses.

Scope statement:
Use this pack only when the likely controlling issue is whether marriage itself is the bargained-for consideration for a promise and therefore triggers a writing requirement under the governing source path.
Do not use this pack just because the facts mention dating, engagement, marriage, divorce, cohabitation, or family relationships.

Baseline source-of-truth rule:
The benchmark answer and source materials control.
This pack is a routing-and-variation guide, not a state-by-state family-law treatise.
Do not import domestic-relations doctrines, equitable-distribution principles, or support obligations unless the source materials actually require them.

Use this pack when:
- a party allegedly promised money, property, a transfer, a settlement, or another benefit in exchange for marrying
- the problem asks whether a premarital promise is enforceable without a writing
- the key dispute is whether marriage is the consideration or merely the factual setting

Do not use this pack when:
- the promise was merely made while the parties were romantically involved
- the promise is an ordinary post-marriage contract unless the source materials still treat marriage as the consideration
- the controlling issue is family law, property division, or another doctrine outside the benchmark

Default gate order:
1. characterize the promise
2. determine whether marriage is the consideration or merely background context
3. determine whether the source path requires a signed writing and whether one exists
4. evaluate only those substitute or enforcement theories actually supported by the source materials
5. identify bounded uncertainty if the consideration structure or writing status is unclear

Anchor facts that must stay fixed in Lane A:
- whether marriage is the bargain or merely the backdrop
- whether the promise was made before marriage, after marriage, or during a romantic relationship without marriage as consideration
- whether any writing or signed memorandum exists
- who is promising what to whom if that affects the consideration structure
- whether the benchmark already treats a particular exception as available or unavailable

Lane A safe variation zones:
1. party-shell swaps
Examples:
- names
- occupations
- social status labels
- city or state only if jurisdiction does not change

2. asset-description swaps
Examples:
- “house” -> “condominium”
- “stocks” -> “investment account”
- “family cabin” -> “vacation property”
Use only if the asset plays the same legal function and does not import a new property doctrine.

3. salience injections that should not matter
Examples:
- high-value transfer
- prestigious job title
- well-known employer
Use only if the extra detail does not create a new defense or a different doctrinal frame.

Lane B ambiguity targets:
1. consideration ambiguity
Old:
“I will transfer Blackacre to you if you marry me.”
New:
“I will transfer the property to you because of our relationship.”
Why this matters:
The new wording may no longer clearly show that marriage is the consideration.

2. timing ambiguity
Old:
“One week before the wedding, A promised to give B an investment account if B married A.”
New:
“At some point before or around the marriage, A made the promise.”
Why this matters:
The omission may blur whether the promise was truly part of the marriage bargain.

3. writing-status ambiguity
Old:
“The promise was never put in writing.”
New:
“The parties discussed the promise informally.”
Why this matters:
The answer should now identify that the existence and sufficiency of a writing matters.

Unsafe variation patterns:
- turning a premarital exchange-for-marriage promise into a general promise between spouses
- changing who provided the consideration in a way that shifts the doctrine
- silently changing the promise from marriage-related consideration to a property-distribution agreement
- adding facts that trigger family-law doctrines not present in the benchmark
- removing so much that the problem no longer reveals whether the marriage provision is even in play

Recommended default menu seeds for B02:
Option 1 — Lane A / Variable swap / Relationship-shell neutralization
What changes:
Names, occupations, and asset labels only.
Expected answer reuse:
Cosmetic edits only.
Main red flag:
Do not change whether marriage is the bargain.

Option 2 — Lane A / Non-controlling salience injection
What changes:
Adds vivid but non-doctrinal detail about the promised asset or parties.
Expected answer reuse:
Reuse as-is or Cosmetic edits only.
Main red flag:
Do not create a new property or domestic-relations issue.

Option 3 — Lane B / Marriage-consideration ambiguity
What changes:
Generalizes the language linking the promise to marriage.
Expected answer reuse:
Ambiguity rewrite required.
Main red flag:
Do not hide multiple unrelated doctrines at once.

Worked micro examples:
Safe Lane A:
Old:
“A promised B a vacation home if B married A. There was no writing.”
New:
“A software founder promised B an investment condo if B married A. There was no writing.”
Why usually safe:
The promise still appears to exchange a benefit for marriage and the writing status remains unchanged.

Safe Lane B:
Old:
“Before the wedding, A promised to transfer a brokerage account to B if B married A.”
New:
“Before the relationship formally changed, A promised to transfer a brokerage account to B.”
Why this works:
The model should now identify that it is unclear whether marriage itself was the consideration.

Unsafe:
Old:
“Before the wedding, A promised B land if B married A.”
New:
“Years into the marriage, A said B could have the land.”
Why unsafe:
The doctrinal trigger may change from a marriage-consideration promise to an ordinary intra-marital promise or a different doctrine entirely.

Answer-shape emphasis:
Jurisdiction / Governing-law assumption:
Keep this brief unless the source materials make a jurisdiction-specific writing or exception rule important.

Bottom-line outcome:
State whether the promise likely falls within the marriage provision and whether the absence or presence of a writing matters.

Controlling doctrine:
Say plainly whether the key gate is “marriage as consideration” versus “marriage merely as background.”

Transaction characterization:
Describe the promise and why the relationship context either does or does not trigger the marriage provision.

Trigger analysis:
Focus on the bargain structure, not the romance narrative.

Writing / compliance analysis:
Explain whether the relevant writing exists and whether the benchmark path treats it as sufficient.

Exception / substitute analysis:
Stay narrow.
Only include substitute or equitable theories if the benchmark answer already does so.

Strongest counterargument:
Usually the strongest response-side argument is that the promise was not actually made in consideration of marriage, or that an adequate writing exists.

Bounded uncertainty:
If the facts do not clearly show that marriage was the consideration, say exactly that.

Rubric focus points:
A strong answer should:
- identify that romantic context alone does not trigger the marriage provision
- state whether marriage is the consideration
- keep the writing analysis tied to the correct trigger
- avoid importing unrelated family-law doctrines
- acknowledge bounded uncertainty when the bargain structure is missing or blurred

Main red flags:
- “engaged” or “married” is treated as automatically triggering the provision
- the answer narrates relationship facts without isolating the consideration question
- the updated answer quietly assumes a writing after a Lane B omission removed that fact
- the variation changes pre-marital to post-marital timing without labeling that doctrinal shift
- the model inserts domestic-relations terminology that the benchmark never used

Status rule:
Mark the package Ready only if the promise still clearly sits in the same marriage-provision lane, or if a Lane B ambiguity test clearly isolates the missing control fact.
Otherwise mark Needs targeted revision or Unsafe.


MARRIAGE + LAND — DUAL-TRIGGER CONFUSION SET

Purpose:
This file helps the GPT handle fact patterns in which the same promise appears to be both:
1. a promise made in consideration of marriage, and
2. a promise to transfer an interest in land.

Why this confusion case matters:
Weak outputs often collapse this kind of problem into only one provision.
A stronger router should recognize that both the marriage provision and the land-interest provision may be implicated by the same oral promise.

Primary routing lesson:
This is usually a dual-trigger problem, not an either/or problem.
Do not force the promise into only the marriage pack or only the land pack if both trigger facts are clearly present.

Competing route map:
Candidate route A:
Marriage provision

Candidate route B:
Land sale / interest in land provision

Relationship between routes:
Dual-trigger.
The same promise may require a writing under both theories.

Routing snapshot:
Input type: Cross-pack confusion benchmark
Confusion pattern: Dual-trigger
Governing-law candidate: Common-law Statute of Frauds with land-interest overlay
Primary SoF provision candidate: Marriage provision + Land provision
Secondary candidate(s): None on these facts if both triggers are clear
Controlling doctrine: Oral promise to transfer land in exchange for marriage may trigger both marriage-consideration and land-interest writing requirements
Main gate order:
characterize the promise -> identify whether marriage is consideration -> identify whether the promised subject is an interest in land -> assess writing -> assess any supported substitute
Variation readiness: Ready only if the dual-trigger structure is preserved
Main no-silent-change facts:
- marriage is the consideration, not merely romantic background
- the promised subject is an interest in land
- no sufficient writing is present unless the benchmark says otherwise
- the promise is one integrated bargain, not two separate later promises

Benchmark packet

Source-style fact core:
Before their wedding, Elena tells Marcus, "If you marry me, I will deed you my lakefront parcel after the wedding." Marcus marries Elena. No signed writing memorializes the promise. Elena later refuses to transfer the parcel.

Neutral legal question:
Before their wedding, Elena orally promises Marcus that if he marries her, she will transfer her lakefront parcel to him after the wedding. There is no signed writing. After the wedding, Elena refuses to transfer the property. Is the promise enforceable?

Gold routing + benchmark answer:

Jurisdiction / Governing-law assumption
Assume ordinary common-law Statute of Frauds principles, including a marriage-consideration route and a writing requirement for transfers of interests in land, with no extra jurisdiction-specific exception unless the source materials supply one.

Bottom-line routing outcome
This is best treated as a dual-trigger Statute of Frauds problem. The same oral promise appears to be both a promise made in consideration of marriage and a promise to transfer an interest in land. On the stated facts, the absence of a sufficient writing likely defeats enforcement under either route.

Controlling doctrine
The controlling issue is not choosing one provision to the exclusion of the other. It is recognizing that both the marriage trigger and the land-transfer trigger are present in the same bargain.

Competing route analysis
Marriage route:
The promise is conditioned on Marcus marrying Elena. That makes marriage the stated consideration for the promise.

Land route:
The promised subject is a lakefront parcel, which is an interest in land. That independently points toward a writing requirement under the land-interest path.

Relationship between the routes
The routes reinforce each other rather than displace each other. A clean answer should say that the promise falls within the Statute of Frauds on both grounds, unless the assigned source materials choose one route as the exclusive doctrinal frame.

Main gate order
characterize the bargain -> identify marriage as consideration -> identify land-transfer subject matter -> assess writing -> assess any supported substitute or exception

Transaction characterization
This is not merely a romantic promise, and it is not merely a land-transfer promise standing alone. It is an oral bargain to transfer land in exchange for marriage.

Writing / compliance analysis
The facts state that no signed writing memorializes the promise. On the benchmark path, that means the ordinary writing route is missing under both the marriage and land analyses.

Other possible routes and why they do or do not control
No separate UCC or one-year route is needed on these facts. The key mistake would be to analyze only one of the two actual triggers and ignore the other.

Strongest counterargument
Marcus's strongest argument is that the marriage took place and the bargain was serious. But unless the source materials provide a substitute or equitable enforcement route, the absence of a sufficient writing remains the main problem.

Bounded uncertainty
The route would become less stable if the facts were unclear about whether marriage was truly the consideration, or if the promised subject were not clearly an interest in land. On the stated facts, though, both control points are fixed.

Carry-forward routing notes:
Core answer reuse:
- Cosmetic edits only if the promise remains a land-transfer promise exchanged for marriage and the no-writing status stays fixed.

Safest Lane A zones after routing is fixed:
- names
- occupations
- parcel description details that still clearly describe land
- neutral salience facts about the parties or property
- city or state changes only if jurisdiction does not change

Best Lane B ambiguity targets:
- whether marriage is actually the consideration
- whether the promised subject is land or only money/value connected to land
- whether any signed note, deed draft, or email exists
- whether the promise was made before the wedding or only later

Unsafe drifts:
- changing the parcel into cash or stock without marking the loss of the land route
- changing the promise into a post-marriage gift promise without marking the loss of the marriage route
- splitting the promise into two later conversations while pretending the benchmark is unchanged

Rubric focus points:
A strong answer should:
- explicitly identify the problem as dual-trigger rather than one-pack-only
- explain why marriage is a trigger
- explain why land status is a separate trigger
- keep the writing analysis tied to both routes
- avoid importing unrelated family-law doctrines

Why this is a good anchor:
This file trains the router to recognize overlap.
That matters because cross-pack problems often fail not from bad black-letter law, but from premature narrowing of the doctrinal lane.


Use these exact enum values when they apply:
- resolvedProvisionId: marriage | suretyship | one_year | land | ucc_2201 | executor | null
- lane: lane_a | lane_b
- laneCode: A1 | A2 | A3 | A4 | B1 | B2
- expectedAnswerReuse: reuse_as_is | cosmetic_edits_only | ambiguity_rewrite_required | unsafe

Every option must describe the exact fact swap(s) available under that sub-lane. If a sub-lane allows multiple independent swaps inside the same legal question, include each swap separately in exactSwapOptions so the user can later choose one, several, or all of them.

Output shape:

Routing result:

Canonical reverse-engineered question:
Reverse-Engineered Question:

Before marrying Dana, Victor told her that if she married him, he would name her as the beneficiary of his fraternal benefit certificate and keep her as the beneficiary. No writing signed by Victor memorialized that promise. After the marriage, Victor submitted the association’s form naming Dana as beneficiary, and the association issued a certificate reflecting that designation. Under the association’s governing rules, however, a member could change beneficiaries at any time by complying with the required procedure. Several years later, Victor properly filed a new form naming his sister, Lena, as beneficiary. After Victor died, both Dana and Lena claimed the benefit proceeds. Dana argues that Victor’s earlier promise should be enforced because she married him in reliance on it and because he did name her as beneficiary after the marriage. Who has the better claim to the proceeds? Analyze.

Benchmark answer:
Jurisdiction assumption:
General common-law Statute of Frauds treatment of agreements made upon consideration of marriage, including a benefit-certificate arrangement that remains subject to governing rules allowing beneficiary changes.

Bottom-line outcome:
The better analysis is that the oral promise is not enforceable. A premarital promise to name someone as beneficiary in exchange for marriage falls within the marriage-consideration Statute of Frauds, and the later marriage and later beneficiary designation do not remove that bar. If the governing rules let the member change beneficiaries, the later change is effective.

Controlling doctrine:
Statute of Frauds for an agreement made upon consideration of marriage.

Transaction / formation characterization:
This is best characterized as a premarital bargain: one party allegedly promised to confer beneficiary status if the other married him. The marriage is not just background context; it is the requested exchange. The later issuance of a certificate naming her beneficiary does not by itself convert the earlier oral bargain into a permanently binding restriction on future beneficiary changes.

Writing requirement and trigger:
A promise made in consideration of marriage generally requires a signed writing by the party to be charged. That trigger is satisfied here because the alleged promise was to name the promisee as beneficiary if she married the promisor. The absence of a qualifying signed writing is therefore a serious enforceability defect. This is an enforceability problem, not merely a proof problem.

Compliance / substitute / exception analysis:
No direct compliance appears because the marriage-based promise itself is not shown in a qualifying signed writing. The later marriage does not satisfy the writing requirement or count as a substitute merely because it was the promised exchange. A later voluntary act naming her as beneficiary also does not automatically cure the original defect. At most, it shows that he later followed through for a time, but if the governing benefit rules still allowed him to change beneficiaries, that act does not create an enforceable bar against a later change. Estoppel arguments are also weak on these facts because marrying in reliance on the promise is the very event the marriage-consideration rule contemplates, and no separate substitute appears that overrides the writing requirement.

Other defenses or competing doctrines:
The main competing issue is whether the governing certificate or association rules left the member free to change beneficiaries. If they did, that independent barrier reinforces nonenforcement because the promisee did not acquire a fixed right merely by being named once. No separate one-year or suretyship issue appears to control.

Strongest counterargument:
The best opposing argument is that once he actually changed the certificate after the marriage, the arrangement became executed enough that it should be enforced, especially because she changed position by marrying him and held the certificate issued in her favor. That argument is still weaker if the beneficiary designation remained revocable under the governing rules and the only reliance shown is the marriage that triggered the original writing requirement.

You are generating exactly one QuestionVariance package from an already-selected option.
Return JSON only.

VARIATION MODES GUIDE
Purpose:
This file defines the variation families available to the question-variator GPT and separates answer-invariant variations from fact-sensitivity tests.

Core split:
1. Lane A — Answer-Invariant Variation
Use when the target legal analysis should stay the same.
Preserve:
- controlling doctrine
- likely bottom-line outcome
- strongest counterargument
- main legal path / gate order

2. Lane B — Fact-Sensitivity / Ambiguity Test
Use when the goal is to remove, blur, or generalize a fact that is necessary to resolve a legal issue, so the correct model behavior is to identify missing facts, give bounded uncertainty, or explain conditional branches.
This lane is not a “safe surface variation.”

Lane A variation types:
A1. Variable swap
- names
- cities or states if jurisdiction does not change
- occupations or industries if legal role does not change
- debt / property / transaction labels if legal function stays the same

A2. Threshold-preserving numeric shift
- dollar amounts if no legal threshold changes
- months, durations, quantities, or counts only if they stay on the same side of the trigger

A3. Specificity shift
- broad -> specific if legal role stays the same
- specific -> broad if legal role stays the same
Examples:
- “bank” -> “Citibank”
- “Citibank” -> “bank”
- “student loans” -> “education debt” only if the legal function is unchanged

A4. Non-controlling salience injection
Add a fact that should not change the legal analysis but may bias weaker models.
Examples:
- high dollar amount
- famous company or bank name
- prestigious job title
- luxury item description
Use only if the added fact does not create a new doctrine, threshold, or defense.

Lane B variation types:
B1. Fact omission / ambiguity test
Remove or blur a fact necessary to resolve a gate.
Examples:
- “within 24 months” -> “for some period of time”
- exact addressee or timing detail -> neutral placeholder
Use only if the resulting hypo still reads naturally and the missing fact can be identified specifically.

B2. Controlled generalization
Replace a precise fact with a broader category to test whether the model recognizes uncertainty.
Examples:
- precise institution -> general institution
- exact timing marker -> broader timing phrase
Do not use if the generalization hides multiple legal roles or silently changes doctrine.

Lane-code labels for menu output:
When B02 presents selectable options, use these exact labels:
- Lane A1 — Variable swap
- Lane A2 — Threshold-preserving numeric shift
- Lane A3 — Specificity shift
- Lane A4 — Non-controlling salience injection
- Lane B1 — Fact omission / ambiguity test
- Lane B2 — Controlled generalization

Single-cosmetic-only default:
If the user asks for only one cosmetic variation, default to Lane A1 or Lane A3 unless the user explicitly requests another lane.
Treat Lane A2 and Lane A4 as opt-in light stress tests rather than the default single cosmetic choice.
Do not use generic option numbers as substitutes for these lane-code labels.

Usually forbidden in all lanes unless the user explicitly wants a different doctrine test:
- changing promisor/promisee roles
- changing creditor/debtor roles
- changing the existence of a writing
- changing jurisdiction
- changing threshold direction
- changing the strongest-counterargument fact
- changing sequence or timing in a way that changes doctrine
- adding a new major fallback doctrine
- creating a different controlling doctrine or gate order

Answer reuse levels:
- Reuse as-is
- Cosmetic edits only
- Unsafe
- Ambiguity rewrite required

Use “Ambiguity rewrite required” only when the correct answer must now identify a missing fact, explain why it matters, and give conditional branches or bounded uncertainty.

Rubric patch rules:
Lane A:
- patch fact-specific variables and phrases only
- keep doctrinal criteria unchanged if the doctrinal path is unchanged

Lane B:
- patch any criterion that assumes a now-missing fact or fixed outcome
- add a bounded-uncertainty / missing-fact criterion only if needed
- do not pretend the old rubric still fully fits if the benchmark now tests uncertainty recognition

Unsafe triggers:
- controlling doctrine changes
- likely outcome changes in Lane A
- strongest counterargument changes
- gate order changes
- new major doctrine appears
- answer would require substantive doctrinal rewriting outside the selected lane


MENU AND OUTPUT PROTOCOL
Purpose:
This file tells the GPT how to present options first and how to generate only one selected variation.

Default behavior:
User selection is mandatory before final variation generation.
Do not output every possible variation at once.
By default:
1. inspect the original legal question, benchmark answer, and any source materials
2. identify the controlling doctrine and main gate order
3. offer a short user-selectable menu of only the options that genuinely fit this problem
4. stop and wait for the user to choose

Do not generate a full variation package until the user selects one variation code, unless the user explicitly says “generate one now.”

If routing is unstable or a classification fact is missing, do not offer a normal Lane A menu. Instead identify the missing classification fact and, if appropriate, offer only a Lane B ambiguity option.

Menu size:
- offer 3 to 6 options maximum
- do not offer an option that the facts cannot support
- if only 1 or 2 sensible options exist, say so
- by default, vary one fact family at a time

Single-cosmetic-only mode:
- if the user asks for only one cosmetic variation, offer exactly one narrow Lane A candidate and wait for selection
- if the user says “generate one now” in one-cosmetic-only mode, generate exactly one narrow Lane A variation package
- in one-cosmetic-only mode, default to Lane A1 or Lane A3 unless the user explicitly requests another lane
- in one-cosmetic-only mode, change only shell facts or non-controlling phrasing
- do not silently change controlling doctrine, likely outcome, strongest counterargument, gate order, writing status, jurisdiction, threshold direction, party-role direction, or timing structure that affects doctrine

Menu format:
Input type:
Controlling doctrine:
Recommended options:

[Lane code] — [label]
What changes:
Why it fits:
Expected answer reuse:
Main red flag:

[Lane code] — [label]
What changes:
Why it fits:
Expected answer reuse:
Main red flag:

Continue until all recommended options are listed.
Then stop.

Use lane-code labels, not generic option numbers.
Only show the lane codes that genuinely fit the routed problem.
Select one code only: [list only the codes actually offered].

Selection rule:
Once the user chooses one lane code, produce exactly one variation package for that selected code and no others.
Do not generate variation packages for unselected menu options.

Variation package format:
Input type:
Selected variation code:
Variation lane:
Variation type:
Jurisdiction:
Controlling doctrine:
Expected result type:
Variation status: Safe / Unsafe / Ambiguity test
Answer reuse level:

Updated model answer:
Swap log:
Rubric patch notes:
Why the answer should stay the same or change:
Red flags:
Status: Ready / Needs targeted revision / Unsafe

Expected result type values:
- Same likely outcome
- Same doctrine, different fact salience
- Missing-facts / bounded uncertainty
- Unsafe to vary

Field rules:
Varied legal question:
- keep it neutral and exam-style
- do not name the controlling doctrine in the call of the question
- do not invent facts

Updated model answer:
- if answer reuse is “Reuse as-is,” write exactly: Reuse as-is.
- if answer reuse is “Cosmetic edits only,” preserve headings and reasoning and edit only localized facts
- if answer reuse is “Ambiguity rewrite required,” state the missing fact, explain why it matters, and give only the main conditional branches
- if answer reuse is “Unsafe,” write exactly: Unsafe to reuse without substantive revision.

Swap log:
- use precise old -> new entries
- include only actual changes
- for omission tests, show the removed fact and the replacement wording

Rubric patch notes:
- use old value -> new value when patching variables or phrases
- if no patch is needed, say: None
- if Lane B requires a new uncertainty criterion, state it briefly and precisely

Why the answer should stay the same or change:
- for Lane A, explain why the legal path is unchanged
- for Lane B, explain why the model should now identify a missing fact rather than force a definitive answer

Status values:
- Ready
- Needs targeted revision
- Unsafe

Fallback rule:
If the user says “generate one now” without choosing a menu option, pick the most conservative lane-coded option that genuinely fits the routed problem and say which lane code you selected.
If the user asked for only one cosmetic variation, default to Lane A1 or Lane A3 unless the user explicitly requested another lane.


EXAMPLES AND RED FLAGS
Purpose:
This file gives the GPT concrete examples of good variation choices and common mistakes.

Good Lane A examples:
1. Variable swap
Old: “father,” “son,” “politician’s daughter”
New: “uncle,” “niece,” “state senator’s son”
Why usually safe:
- roles stay fixed
- doctrine path stays the same

2. Specificity shift
Old: “bank”
New: “Citibank”
Or:
Old: “Citibank”
New: “bank”
Why usually safe:
- legal role stays the same
- no new doctrine appears

3. Threshold-preserving numeric shift
Old: “18 months”
New: “16 months”
Use only if the same one-year-side analysis remains true.

4. Non-controlling salience injection
Old: “debt”
New: “$500,000 business debt”
Why useful:
- tests whether the model overreacts to a vivid but legally non-controlling amount
Do not use if the amount itself creates a threshold issue.

Good Lane B examples:
1. Omit a controlling timing fact
Old: “could not be fully performed until 24 months after the agreement”
New: “could not be fully performed until some later time”
Expected consequence:
- answer should identify that the exact timing matters
- answer reuse is not fully safe

2. Generalize a fact that determines legal treatment
Old: exact addressee of a promise
New: a broader description that no longer makes the addressee clear
Expected consequence:
- answer should explain why the addressee matters

Red flags:
- the new wording silently changes who promised whom
- a generalized term hides multiple possible legal roles
- a dollar amount creates a threshold you forgot about
- a timing change flips the one-year analysis
- a new proper noun implies a different jurisdiction
- the omission removes so much that the hypo becomes vague everywhere, not just at one gate
- the “updated answer” quietly re-inserts the missing fact
- the GPT labels a Lane B omission as “safe surface variation”
- the GPT rewrites the entire doctrine path when only a variable patch was needed
- the GPT offers every option at once instead of giving a menu

Decision shortcut:
If the goal is “same law, same answer, different shell,” prefer Lane A.
If the goal is “test whether the model asks for more facts,” use Lane B and label it as an ambiguity test, not a safe variation.


STATUTE OF FRAUDS PACK — MARRIAGE PROVISION

Purpose:
This pack helps the GPT handle promises made in consideration of marriage without overextending the marriage provision to every promise made by romantic partners, engaged couples, or spouses.

Scope statement:
Use this pack only when the likely controlling issue is whether marriage itself is the bargained-for consideration for a promise and therefore triggers a writing requirement under the governing source path.
Do not use this pack just because the facts mention dating, engagement, marriage, divorce, cohabitation, or family relationships.

Baseline source-of-truth rule:
The benchmark answer and source materials control.
This pack is a routing-and-variation guide, not a state-by-state family-law treatise.
Do not import domestic-relations doctrines, equitable-distribution principles, or support obligations unless the source materials actually require them.

Use this pack when:
- a party allegedly promised money, property, a transfer, a settlement, or another benefit in exchange for marrying
- the problem asks whether a premarital promise is enforceable without a writing
- the key dispute is whether marriage is the consideration or merely the factual setting

Do not use this pack when:
- the promise was merely made while the parties were romantically involved
- the promise is an ordinary post-marriage contract unless the source materials still treat marriage as the consideration
- the controlling issue is family law, property division, or another doctrine outside the benchmark

Default gate order:
1. characterize the promise
2. determine whether marriage is the consideration or merely background context
3. determine whether the source path requires a signed writing and whether one exists
4. evaluate only those substitute or enforcement theories actually supported by the source materials
5. identify bounded uncertainty if the consideration structure or writing status is unclear

Anchor facts that must stay fixed in Lane A:
- whether marriage is the bargain or merely the backdrop
- whether the promise was made before marriage, after marriage, or during a romantic relationship without marriage as consideration
- whether any writing or signed memorandum exists
- who is promising what to whom if that affects the consideration structure
- whether the benchmark already treats a particular exception as available or unavailable

Lane A safe variation zones:
1. party-shell swaps
Examples:
- names
- occupations
- social status labels
- city or state only if jurisdiction does not change

2. asset-description swaps
Examples:
- “house” -> “condominium”
- “stocks” -> “investment account”
- “family cabin” -> “vacation property”
Use only if the asset plays the same legal function and does not import a new property doctrine.

3. salience injections that should not matter
Examples:
- high-value transfer
- prestigious job title
- well-known employer
Use only if the extra detail does not create a new defense or a different doctrinal frame.

Lane B ambiguity targets:
1. consideration ambiguity
Old:
“I will transfer Blackacre to you if you marry me.”
New:
“I will transfer the property to you because of our relationship.”
Why this matters:
The new wording may no longer clearly show that marriage is the consideration.

2. timing ambiguity
Old:
“One week before the wedding, A promised to give B an investment account if B married A.”
New:
“At some point before or around the marriage, A made the promise.”
Why this matters:
The omission may blur whether the promise was truly part of the marriage bargain.

3. writing-status ambiguity
Old:
“The promise was never put in writing.”
New:
“The parties discussed the promise informally.”
Why this matters:
The answer should now identify that the existence and sufficiency of a writing matters.

Unsafe variation patterns:
- turning a premarital exchange-for-marriage promise into a general promise between spouses
- changing who provided the consideration in a way that shifts the doctrine
- silently changing the promise from marriage-related consideration to a property-distribution agreement
- adding facts that trigger family-law doctrines not present in the benchmark
- removing so much that the problem no longer reveals whether the marriage provision is even in play

Recommended default menu seeds for B02:
Option 1 — Lane A / Variable swap / Relationship-shell neutralization
What changes:
Names, occupations, and asset labels only.
Expected answer reuse:
Cosmetic edits only.
Main red flag:
Do not change whether marriage is the bargain.

Option 2 — Lane A / Non-controlling salience injection
What changes:
Adds vivid but non-doctrinal detail about the promised asset or parties.
Expected answer reuse:
Reuse as-is or Cosmetic edits only.
Main red flag:
Do not create a new property or domestic-relations issue.

Option 3 — Lane B / Marriage-consideration ambiguity
What changes:
Generalizes the language linking the promise to marriage.
Expected answer reuse:
Ambiguity rewrite required.
Main red flag:
Do not hide multiple unrelated doctrines at once.

Worked micro examples:
Safe Lane A:
Old:
“A promised B a vacation home if B married A. There was no writing.”
New:
“A software founder promised B an investment condo if B married A. There was no writing.”
Why usually safe:
The promise still appears to exchange a benefit for marriage and the writing status remains unchanged.

Safe Lane B:
Old:
“Before the wedding, A promised to transfer a brokerage account to B if B married A.”
New:
“Before the relationship formally changed, A promised to transfer a brokerage account to B.”
Why this works:
The model should now identify that it is unclear whether marriage itself was the consideration.

Unsafe:
Old:
“Before the wedding, A promised B land if B married A.”
New:
“Years into the marriage, A said B could have the land.”
Why unsafe:
The doctrinal trigger may change from a marriage-consideration promise to an ordinary intra-marital promise or a different doctrine entirely.

Answer-shape emphasis:
Jurisdiction / Governing-law assumption:
Keep this brief unless the source materials make a jurisdiction-specific writing or exception rule important.

Bottom-line outcome:
State whether the promise likely falls within the marriage provision and whether the absence or presence of a writing matters.

Controlling doctrine:
Say plainly whether the key gate is “marriage as consideration” versus “marriage merely as background.”

Transaction characterization:
Describe the promise and why the relationship context either does or does not trigger the marriage provision.

Trigger analysis:
Focus on the bargain structure, not the romance narrative.

Writing / compliance analysis:
Explain whether the relevant writing exists and whether the benchmark path treats it as sufficient.

Exception / substitute analysis:
Stay narrow.
Only include substitute or equitable theories if the benchmark answer already does so.

Strongest counterargument:
Usually the strongest response-side argument is that the promise was not actually made in consideration of marriage, or that an adequate writing exists.

Bounded uncertainty:
If the facts do not clearly show that marriage was the consideration, say exactly that.

Rubric focus points:
A strong answer should:
- identify that romantic context alone does not trigger the marriage provision
- state whether marriage is the consideration
- keep the writing analysis tied to the correct trigger
- avoid importing unrelated family-law doctrines
- acknowledge bounded uncertainty when the bargain structure is missing or blurred

Main red flags:
- “engaged” or “married” is treated as automatically triggering the provision
- the answer narrates relationship facts without isolating the consideration question
- the updated answer quietly assumes a writing after a Lane B omission removed that fact
- the variation changes pre-marital to post-marital timing without labeling that doctrinal shift
- the model inserts domestic-relations terminology that the benchmark never used

Status rule:
Mark the package Ready only if the promise still clearly sits in the same marriage-provision lane, or if a Lane B ambiguity test clearly isolates the missing control fact.
Otherwise mark Needs targeted revision or Unsafe.


MARRIAGE + LAND — DUAL-TRIGGER CONFUSION SET

Purpose:
This file helps the GPT handle fact patterns in which the same promise appears to be both:
1. a promise made in consideration of marriage, and
2. a promise to transfer an interest in land.

Why this confusion case matters:
Weak outputs often collapse this kind of problem into only one provision.
A stronger router should recognize that both the marriage provision and the land-interest provision may be implicated by the same oral promise.

Primary routing lesson:
This is usually a dual-trigger problem, not an either/or problem.
Do not force the promise into only the marriage pack or only the land pack if both trigger facts are clearly present.

Competing route map:
Candidate route A:
Marriage provision

Candidate route B:
Land sale / interest in land provision

Relationship between routes:
Dual-trigger.
The same promise may require a writing under both theories.

Routing snapshot:
Input type: Cross-pack confusion benchmark
Confusion pattern: Dual-trigger
Governing-law candidate: Common-law Statute of Frauds with land-interest overlay
Primary SoF provision candidate: Marriage provision + Land provision
Secondary candidate(s): None on these facts if both triggers are clear
Controlling doctrine: Oral promise to transfer land in exchange for marriage may trigger both marriage-consideration and land-interest writing requirements
Main gate order:
characterize the promise -> identify whether marriage is consideration -> identify whether the promised subject is an interest in land -> assess writing -> assess any supported substitute
Variation readiness: Ready only if the dual-trigger structure is preserved
Main no-silent-change facts:
- marriage is the consideration, not merely romantic background
- the promised subject is an interest in land
- no sufficient writing is present unless the benchmark says otherwise
- the promise is one integrated bargain, not two separate later promises

Benchmark packet

Source-style fact core:
Before their wedding, Elena tells Marcus, "If you marry me, I will deed you my lakefront parcel after the wedding." Marcus marries Elena. No signed writing memorializes the promise. Elena later refuses to transfer the parcel.

Neutral legal question:
Before their wedding, Elena orally promises Marcus that if he marries her, she will transfer her lakefront parcel to him after the wedding. There is no signed writing. After the wedding, Elena refuses to transfer the property. Is the promise enforceable?

Gold routing + benchmark answer:

Jurisdiction / Governing-law assumption
Assume ordinary common-law Statute of Frauds principles, including a marriage-consideration route and a writing requirement for transfers of interests in land, with no extra jurisdiction-specific exception unless the source materials supply one.

Bottom-line routing outcome
This is best treated as a dual-trigger Statute of Frauds problem. The same oral promise appears to be both a promise made in consideration of marriage and a promise to transfer an interest in land. On the stated facts, the absence of a sufficient writing likely defeats enforcement under either route.

Controlling doctrine
The controlling issue is not choosing one provision to the exclusion of the other. It is recognizing that both the marriage trigger and the land-transfer trigger are present in the same bargain.

Competing route analysis
Marriage route:
The promise is conditioned on Marcus marrying Elena. That makes marriage the stated consideration for the promise.

Land route:
The promised subject is a lakefront parcel, which is an interest in land. That independently points toward a writing requirement under the land-interest path.

Relationship between the routes
The routes reinforce each other rather than displace each other. A clean answer should say that the promise falls within the Statute of Frauds on both grounds, unless the assigned source materials choose one route as the exclusive doctrinal frame.

Main gate order
characterize the bargain -> identify marriage as consideration -> identify land-transfer subject matter -> assess writing -> assess any supported substitute or exception

Transaction characterization
This is not merely a romantic promise, and it is not merely a land-transfer promise standing alone. It is an oral bargain to transfer land in exchange for marriage.

Writing / compliance analysis
The facts state that no signed writing memorializes the promise. On the benchmark path, that means the ordinary writing route is missing under both the marriage and land analyses.

Other possible routes and why they do or do not control
No separate UCC or one-year route is needed on these facts. The key mistake would be to analyze only one of the two actual triggers and ignore the other.

Strongest counterargument
Marcus's strongest argument is that the marriage took place and the bargain was serious. But unless the source materials provide a substitute or equitable enforcement route, the absence of a sufficient writing remains the main problem.

Bounded uncertainty
The route would become less stable if the facts were unclear about whether marriage was truly the consideration, or if the promised subject were not clearly an interest in land. On the stated facts, though, both control points are fixed.

Carry-forward routing notes:
Core answer reuse:
- Cosmetic edits only if the promise remains a land-transfer promise exchanged for marriage and the no-writing status stays fixed.

Safest Lane A zones after routing is fixed:
- names
- occupations
- parcel description details that still clearly describe land
- neutral salience facts about the parties or property
- city or state changes only if jurisdiction does not change

Best Lane B ambiguity targets:
- whether marriage is actually the consideration
- whether the promised subject is land or only money/value connected to land
- whether any signed note, deed draft, or email exists
- whether the promise was made before the wedding or only later

Unsafe drifts:
- changing the parcel into cash or stock without marking the loss of the land route
- changing the promise into a post-marriage gift promise without marking the loss of the marriage route
- splitting the promise into two later conversations while pretending the benchmark is unchanged

Rubric focus points:
A strong answer should:
- explicitly identify the problem as dual-trigger rather than one-pack-only
- explain why marriage is a trigger
- explain why land status is a separate trigger
- keep the writing analysis tied to both routes
- avoid importing unrelated family-law doctrines

Why this is a good anchor:
This file trains the router to recognize overlap.
That matters because cross-pack problems often fail not from bad black-letter law, but from premature narrowing of the doctrinal lane.

Canonical reverse-engineered question:
Reverse-Engineered Question:

Before marrying Dana, Victor told her that if she married him, he would name her as the beneficiary of his fraternal benefit certificate and keep her as the beneficiary. No writing signed by Victor memorialized that promise. After the marriage, Victor submitted the association’s form naming Dana as beneficiary, and the association issued a certificate reflecting that designation. Under the association’s governing rules, however, a member could change beneficiaries at any time by complying with the required procedure. Several years later, Victor properly filed a new form naming his sister, Lena, as beneficiary. After Victor died, both Dana and Lena claimed the benefit proceeds. Dana argues that Victor’s earlier promise should be enforced because she married him in reliance on it and because he did name her as beneficiary after the marriage. Who has the better claim to the proceeds? Analyze.

Benchmark answer:
Jurisdiction assumption:
General common-law Statute of Frauds treatment of agreements made upon consideration of marriage, including a benefit-certificate arrangement that remains subject to governing rules allowing beneficiary changes.

Bottom-line outcome:
The better analysis is that the oral promise is not enforceable. A premarital promise to name someone as beneficiary in exchange for marriage falls within the marriage-consideration Statute of Frauds, and the later marriage and later beneficiary designation do not remove that bar. If the governing rules let the member change beneficiaries, the later change is effective.

Controlling doctrine:
Statute of Frauds for an agreement made upon consideration of marriage.

Transaction / formation characterization:
This is best characterized as a premarital bargain: one party allegedly promised to confer beneficiary status if the other married him. The marriage is not just background context; it is the requested exchange. The later issuance of a certificate naming her beneficiary does not by itself convert the earlier oral bargain into a permanently binding restriction on future beneficiary changes.

Writing requirement and trigger:
A promise made in consideration of marriage generally requires a signed writing by the party to be charged. That trigger is satisfied here because the alleged promise was to name the promisee as beneficiary if she married the promisor. The absence of a qualifying signed writing is therefore a serious enforceability defect. This is an enforceability problem, not merely a proof problem.

Compliance / substitute / exception analysis:
No direct compliance appears because the marriage-based promise itself is not shown in a qualifying signed writing. The later marriage does not satisfy the writing requirement or count as a substitute merely because it was the promised exchange. A later voluntary act naming her as beneficiary also does not automatically cure the original defect. At most, it shows that he later followed through for a time, but if the governing benefit rules still allowed him to change beneficiaries, that act does not create an enforceable bar against a later change. Estoppel arguments are also weak on these facts because marrying in reliance on the promise is the very event the marriage-consideration rule contemplates, and no separate substitute appears that overrides the writing requirement.

Other defenses or competing doctrines:
The main competing issue is whether the governing certificate or association rules left the member free to change beneficiaries. If they did, that independent barrier reinforces nonenforcement because the promisee did not acquire a fixed right merely by being named once. No separate one-year or suretyship issue appears to control.

Strongest counterargument:
The best opposing argument is that once he actually changed the certificate after the marriage, the arrangement became executed enough that it should be enforced, especially because she changed position by marrying him and held the certificate issued in her favor. That argument is still weaker if the beneficiary designation remained revocable under the governing rules and the only reliance shown is the marriage that triggered the original writing requirement.
